books.title: The Old Man and the Sea
books.author: Ernest Hemingway
books.context: I came to this after abandoning Henry James at chapter 15. The Portrait of a Lady left me cold — characters hooked to their ideas, nobody real, nobody moving. I needed a calibration reset. A man alone with his craft. No noise. This is what I found.

themes.title: Age as clarification
themes.body: I keep returning to what gets stripped away with age and what stays. Pride goes first — it becomes a luxury I can no longer afford. Humility is not weakness; it is the actual legs I walk on now. When I read about the old man's eyes — unchanged, cheerful, undefeated — I felt seen. The soul holds its life better than the flesh does.

themes.title: Memory and distillation
themes.body: I question my own memories the older I get. Did I remember that, or did someone tell me? But I have come to think accuracy is not really the point. What matters is what gets distilled — what remains after the rest falls away. That is what memoir is, for me. Not the record. The residue.

themes.title: Dreams and ownership
themes.body: The old man no longer dreams of storms or women or great fish. He dreams of places. I read that and thought: this is what maturity looks like in the interior life. No more wanting things that can be owned. The bigger themes — those can never be owned, only shared.

themes.title: The Swiss knife of age
themes.body: I made a list of what the old man actually has: luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy, and lions in dreams. That is not a catalogue of losses. That is a toolkit. I recognise most of those things in myself now, and I did not have them when I was young.

themes.title: The big fish
themes.body: Would he go out at all if there were no fish to hope for? I did not answer that when I read it. I still have not answered it. It is the question underneath everything — about what keeps a person moving, what faith actually is when you strip the word down.

entries.quote: Everything about him was old except his eyes and they were the same color as the sea and were cheerful and undefeated.
entries.response: And the eyes are the doors to the soul, it changes and matures but it holds its life better than the flesh..

entries.quote: Can you really remember that or did I just tell it to you? / I remember everything from when we first went together.
entries.response: The younger you are, the more sure about you memories. The older I become I question the memory from the memoirs.. but does it matter?! Not really, it's what is distilled that matters..

entries.quote: Thank you, the old man said. He was too simple to wonder when he had attained humility. But he knew he had attained it and he knew it was not disgraceful...
entries.response: Wow.. how true.. Pride is a luxury for the young, humility the legs of aging.. life do nibble on pride, a survival mechanism is it?!

entries.quote: He no longer dreamed of storms, nor of women, nor of great occurrences, nor of great fish, nor fights, nor contests of strength, nor of his wife. He only dreamed of places...
entries.response: Of man and his dreams.. no more for wants and ownerships.. it's dreams of the bigger themes that can never be owned, but shared.

entries.quote: My big fish must be somewhere.
entries.response: The hope of a big fish, would he go out if it's none..

entries.quote: I am a strange old man. / But are you strong enough now for a truly big fish? / I think so. And there are many tricks.
entries.response: Let's count the Swiss knife tools of the aged as Hemingway thinks; luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy and lions in dreams.

entries.title: Photo, 1958
entries.raw:
I sat cross-legged on the floor of my Kuwait apartment, surrounded by the ghosts of my family history. The photographs—curled at the edges, faded in places—formed a timeline of departures, an atlas of separations. My fingers hovered over them, reluctant to disturb their order, as if the slightest movement might unravel the thread that connected me to these moments I'd never witnessed.

Outside, the evening prayer call warbled through the cooling air, a mournful counterpoint to the persistent hum of my air conditioner. The light filtering through my curtains had softened to amber, casting long shadows across the wooden floor where I'd arranged my inheritance of memories. The neighbors' children squealed somewhere in the distance, their voices floating up five stories, sounding both present and impossibly far away—like the past itself.

My hand finally settled on a black-and-white photograph, slightly smaller than the others. Its edges were crisp, protected by decades spent pressed between the pages of a leather-bound Quran. This one always drew me first—my father at twenty-six, standing on a Cairo street in 1959, three days before he would leave for Kuwait. He looked impossibly young, his posture stiff with formality, his thin mustache perfectly trimmed. The camera had caught him in profile, his gaze fixed on something beyond the frame, something in the direction of the Gulf.

In the photograph, his right hand clutched the handle of a small suitcase, his knuckles visibly tight even through the grain of the old print. His left hand rested on the lapel of his suit jacket—a gesture of reassurance, perhaps, or just nervousness disguised as formality. Behind him, Cairo existed as a blur of white buildings and utility poles, the city already receding before he'd even left it.

I knew the story of this photograph by heart—how my uncle had insisted on taking it, how my father had complained about the sun in his eyes, how the neighborhood children had gathered to watch the spectacle of a man being photographed in his best suit on an ordinary Tuesday. My father rarely spoke of his departure, but when he did, it was always about the packing.

"Never have I packed with such precision," he once told me over bitter coffee, many years later. "Each thing in its place, as if creating order in my suitcase could create order in my life."

I imagined him in his Cairo apartment, folding each item with monastic focus. His dental tools came first—the small picks and sculptors wrapped individually in squares of soft white cloth, then bundled together in a roll tied with string. These were not merely tools but extensions of himself, the implements that would transform him from a Cairo boy to a Kuwait man. I'd seen how he handled them even decades later, with a reverence usually reserved for religious artifacts.

The letters of recommendation had been tucked carefully into a leather portfolio, a gift from his father. Three letters—one from his instructor at the technical school, one from the clinic where he'd apprenticed, and one from a distant cousin who knew someone in the Kuwait Ministry of Health. Each letter had been read and reread until its creases threatened to tear the paper, each official stamp and signature studied like a map to a promised land.

His clothes had been few: two shirts besides the one he wore for travel, a spare pair of trousers, undergarments, socks. "What use are many clothes?" he'd say whenever my mother tried to expand his wardrobe. "A man needs only enough to be clean and presentable." This minimalism wasn't philosophy but necessity transformed into virtue—he simply couldn't carry more across the distance he intended to travel.

The suitcase itself had been borrowed from a neighbor. Small, made of cardboard covered with imitation leather, it had a broken lock that my father secured with a belt. Inside its lid, a previous owner had pasted a picture of Paris, a city my father would never see. He'd kept that suitcase under his bed in Kuwait for years, empty but unavailable for any other purpose, as if it remained assigned to some future journey.

I leaned closer to the photograph, studying the shadows under my father's eyes. Were they from the late afternoon sun, or had he slept poorly in the nights before leaving? His mouth was set in a line that wasn't quite a frown, his jaw clenched slightly—the face of a man steeling himself. I recognized that expression; I'd seen it in the mirror thirty years later, when preparing for my own departure.

There was something else in the image I hadn't noticed before—the way he leaned almost imperceptibly toward his destination, his weight shifted forward onto the balls of his feet. He was literally poised for flight, already leaving even as the camera captured him. I wondered if he knew then what pattern he was setting in motion, what invisible tracks he was laying down for me to follow decades later.

I set the photograph aside and picked up another—my mother at seventeen, clutching her wedding dress at an airport. The parallels made my throat tighten. Pattern recognition was supposedly a sign of intelligence, but sometimes it felt more like a curse. How many of our choices are truly our own, and how many are echoes of decisions made before we were born? The thought left me uneasy, sitting there among the scattered evidence of repeated history.

The prayer call faded, leaving behind a silence that seemed to emphasize my solitude. I gathered the photographs carefully, returning them to their box—all except my father's, which I propped against the lamp on my bedside table. Tomorrow I would begin my own packing. I wondered what photographs would be taken of me, what future eyes might study them for clues about who I was and why I left. What would they see that I couldn't? What patterns would I set in motion that I couldn't yet recognize?

I stood up, joints stiff from sitting too long, and went to find my own small suitcase in the back of the closet. Unlike my father, I owned one. Unlike him, I'd always known I would need it someday.
entries.elevated:
I sat cross-legged on the cool wooden floor of my Kuwait apartment, the evening light filtering amber through my curtains as I arranged the scattered archaeology of my family's departures. The photographs—their edges soft with age, some corners lifting like small wings—formed a chronology of leavings that stretched back decades. Outside, the call to prayer drifted through the humid air, its familiar cadence mixing with the distant laughter of children playing in the courtyard below and the steady hum of my air conditioner fighting against the Gulf heat.

My fingers found the photograph they always sought first: my father at twenty-six, captured on a Cairo street in 1959, three days before his journey to Kuwait would begin. The black-and-white image had been preserved between the pages of a leather Quran, its edges still crisp while others had curled and faded. He stood in profile, impossibly young, his thin mustache perfectly groomed, his hand gripping a small borrowed suitcase with visible tension. Behind him, Cairo blurred into white buildings and tangled utility wires—a city already becoming memory.

I knew this moment by heart: how my uncle had insisted on the photograph, how my father had squinted against the afternoon sun, how neighborhood children had gathered to watch a man pose in his best suit on an ordinary Tuesday. "Never have I packed with such precision," he once told me over bitter coffee years later, his voice carrying the weight of that careful preparation. I could see it in his posture—the way he leaned almost imperceptibly forward, already in motion toward a future he couldn't yet imagine, laying down the invisible tracks I would follow thirty years later.

Sitting there in the gathering dusk, surrounded by these captured moments of departure, I felt the familiar tightness in my throat that comes with pattern recognition. Tomorrow I would begin my own packing, and somewhere in that rhythm of folding and choosing and leaving behind, I wondered what future eyes might study photographs of me, searching for clues about why we leave, why we follow the paths our parents carved in the air before us.
questions: Do you remember the specific bitter coffee moment when your father told you about his precise packing—where were you, what prompted him to share that memory?
What made you decide to prop that particular photograph against your bedside lamp that night instead of returning it to the box with the others?
When you found your own suitcase in the closet, did it feel different from your father's borrowed one—what did that difference mean to you in that moment?

reflection.text:
I'm starting to see Santiago not as some tragic figure, but as someone who's figured out something I'm still wrestling with. When I first read about his eyes being "cheerful and undefeated," I thought it was just poetic description. But now, six passages in, I realize Hemingway is showing me what survives when everything else gets stripped away. Santiago has lost his strength, his luck, his youth - hell, even the kid can't fish with him anymore - but there's this core that remains untouched. It's not stubbornness; it's something deeper.

What's catching me off guard is how Santiago's relationship with memory and dreams has evolved. He doesn't question whether his memories are real anymore, and his dreams have shifted from wanting things to simply being in places. There's something profoundly liberating about that transition that I didn't expect to find moving. I keep thinking about my own dreams lately - am I still dreaming of contests and acquisitions, or am I starting to dream of spaces, of being rather than having?

The humility passage hit me hardest. "Too simple to wonder when he had attained humility" - that phrase keeps rolling around in my head. I've always thought of humility as something you work toward, analyze, achieve consciously. But Santiago just inhabits it naturally now, without the self-consciousness that would make it performative. It makes me wonder if I'm overthinking my own growth, if real wisdom comes from this kind of unconscious settling into what you've become.

And yet he still believes in his big fish. That's what's keeping me hooked - this isn't a story about resignation or quiet defeat. Santiago has all these tools now, this "Swiss knife of the aged" as I put it, but he's still out there hunting for something magnificent. The combination of deep acceptance and persistent hope feels like a paradox I need to understand better. Maybe that's what undefeated eyes actually look like.

books.title: The Heart's Invisible Furies
books.author: John Boyne
books.context: Recommended to me as East of Eden crossed with Covenant of Water — modern, sharper, faster. Life across decades. Identity, family, exile, belonging. I started it after Hemingway.

entries.quote: The Good People of Goleen Long before we discovered that he had fathered two children by two different women, one in Drimoleague and one in Cl...
entries.response: I think this has to count as one of the most memorable first paragraph of a novel .. it starts with a sarcastic title of a chapter , followed by the act of a sinful priest and ends with a mother called a whore!. The Good People of Goleen - capitalised-but no one seems to be Good.

entries.quote: "Good luck to you now." And with that she took her leave of Goleen, the place of her birth, which she would not see again for more than sixty years, whe...
entries.response: The ending paragraph, tells us with what to come .. the cast away will return and probably nothing would have changed in the village but for the occupied graveyard housing the people who shunned her 60 years earlier. The whole chapter feels more comic than tragic, the humour echoes the style of Frank McCourt in Angela's ashes.

You keep wondering about the meaning of this title until  half way in this chapter when Sean McIntyre introduces himself as the bad lad from out near Ballincollig, somewhere in Cork County where it gets to your head if you live there long enough. The chapter takes the two youngsters fleeing from their own miseries to a freedom that is yet to materialise. Both share tender exchange of their misfortune as they head to the Big Smoke in Dublin. The bus ride is their transition from one life to another and it is a nice way of introducing the two characters to us .

The author keeps referring to Catherine as my mother . The frequency of this is stark , why , it even feels weird considering the speaker is still an unborn baby who is a non witness ... is it a love word ..

The chapter started with a cast away girl , reluctant to talk to a stranger , to pouring her secrets to him and ending with a possible career as a shop girl. The author pointed how beautiful she is and he is handsome but discarded the brief idea of loving birds for a partners in fight and flight.

Two toddlers stories come to mind ; one of "The three little duckling" It's a story of yearning for Adventure and at the same time being happy to return to the comfort of Home. The second story of "The Ugly duckling" who is shunned by her siblings for he was different , he leaves home and returns as a wonderful swan . Is this the fate of the authors mother ? , would she return home triumphant .

entries.quote: "Hmm," he said, striding ahead, leaving Seán and my mother to follow.
entries.response: By the end of this scene where Smoot get aquatinted with Catherine for the first time n their way from the bus station to the flat on Catham street; we see the contrast between the three -I guess- to be friends; Catherine , the impulsive optimist, Sean the kind easy going and Smoot the dark and suspicious pessimist .

reflection.text:
I'm starting to see how Boyne is weaving these character archetypes together, and it's more sophisticated than I initially gave him credit for. Catherine as the impulsive optimist, Sean as the kind mediator, and now Smoot as this dark, suspicious force - they feel like they're going to represent different responses to the trauma of being outcasts. What strikes me is how Catherine maintains this almost naive hope despite everything that's happened to her. There's something both beautiful and heartbreaking about that resilience.

The more I think about those fairy tale echoes I noted - the three ducklings, the ugly duckling - the more I'm convinced Boyne is deliberately playing with these childhood stories we all know, but subverting them. This isn't going to be a simple tale of the ugly duckling becoming a swan. The world he's building is too complex, too rooted in the specific cruelties of 1940s Ireland, for such easy redemption. Yet there's still that thread of hope running through it all.

I'm becoming obsessed with that repeated "my mother" refrain. The more I encounter it, the more it feels like a kind of prayer or incantation. This unborn narrator isn't just telling a story - he's claiming her, protecting her memory, insisting on her worth in a world that discarded her. It's love, yes, but it's also defiance. Every time he says "my mother," he's pushing back against everyone who called her a whore, everyone who cast her out.

The bus journey chapter really crystallized something for me about Boyne's approach to trauma. He doesn't dwell in the pain - he shows us these characters in motion, always moving toward something, even when they don't know what that something is. Catherine and Sean sharing their stories on that bus, finding connection in their shared exile - it's such a perfect metaphor for how we survive the worst things that happen to us. We find each other, we keep moving, and sometimes we even find reasons to laugh.

books.title: Memoir
books.author: Tarek Abdel Tawab
books.context: A story of the patterns that revealed themselves with time—how lives echo across generations, how choices circle back in altered shapes, and how I found myself, almost unknowingly, stepping into paths already waiting for me.
